返回本批次

为第五元素创造未来景观（2019）

Creating the Futurescape for the Fifth Element (2019)

theasc.com 作者 nixass 分数 118 评论 97 2026/04/09 17:11
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 如果说《星球大战》特别版的票房大卖证明了什么，那就是观众再次渴望观看纯粹主义者曾经轻蔑地称为“太空歌剧”的东西。然而，法国导演吕克·贝松的大胆新片《第五元素》与其说归功于乔治·卢卡斯，不如说归功于让-吕克·戈达尔的荒诞主义情感。同样，这部电影的强烈意象部分是由法国艺术家让·“莫比斯”·吉罗（高卢图形杂志 Metal Hurlant 的）和让-克洛德·梅齐埃（《星际特工：时空代理人》）构思的，他们与美术指导丹·韦尔一起帮助贝松将一部导演培育了近 20 年的奇幻电影形象化。一路走来，电影制片人完善并扩展了他的想法，涵盖了过去二十年的每一项特效创新。
  2. 本文最初发表于 1997 年 5 月的 AC。有些照片是附加的或备用的。结果是一本视觉上令人惊叹的、生动的漫画书，在一个完全现实的星系中播放——从各个方向延伸 600 个故事的纽约（还有飞行汽车造成的交通堵塞）到一艘在水星球上空巡航的豪华外太空游船。第五元素对未来的深远愿景需要视觉效果的虚拟“采样器”，贝松选择了数字王国来实现这一切。大约 85 名模型制作者和 85 名艺术家一次性完成了影片的 220 多个特效镜头。领头羊是首次担任视觉效果总监的马克·斯泰森 (Mark Stetson)。斯泰森曾是一名模型制作人（《银翼杀手》、《哈德吸盘代理人》），在为 Waterworld 完成了命运多舛的埃克森·瓦尔迪兹号的巨大微缩模型后，他关闭了自己的工作室 Stetson Visual Services。四个月内，他加入了数字王国，制作了《第五元素》。视觉效果制片人丹·隆巴多（《莫罗博士之岛》）在评估两人在片场的关系时指出，“卢克是那种对制作的各个方面都非常亲力亲为的导演。在这部剧中，他并没有委托太多事情，除非涉及到他不太了解的事情。那时他会向马克倾斜并要求他介入。”斯泰森本人肯定道：“与吕克建立这种关系真是太棒了，他通常非常注重隐私和保护欲。这部电影的设计深受七十年代法国漫画书外观的影响，这使得制作起来非常有趣。虽然其中一部分发生在未来派的纽约市，但我认为场景与《银翼杀手》中的洛杉矶有很大不同。飞行汽车更加异想天开，而且城市背景是在光天化日之下。这部电影植根于一个比《银翼杀手》更加乌托邦式的未来愿景，十多年来，《银翼杀手》几乎定义了未来电影的后世界末日面貌。” 《第五元素》以 20 世纪初的埃及为背景，一艘由名为 Mondoshawan 的神圣外星人种族驾驶的宇宙飞船降落到地球。这艘外星飞船是海螺壳和足球的令人愉悦的锈红色混合体，由 Metal Hurlant 艺术家 Moebius 设计，并由 Digital Domain 模型店制作成 8 英寸微缩模型；来自 NASA 照片的数字哑光绘画，为地球加倍。足球形状的 Mondoshawan 工艺品正在接受考验。抵达后，椭圆形蒙多沙万飞船悬停在一座奇特的岩石神庙上方的能量束上。制作人员在一块从干涸的湖床上升起的巨大岩石上画了一个门口，将其改造成寺庙的外观。大部分底片由贝松和第五元素电影摄影师蒂埃里·阿博加斯特 (AFC) 拍摄，数字王国视觉效果摄影总监比尔·尼尔 (Bill Neil) 担任顾问。
  3. 在担任了十年的摄像助理和摄像师之后，尼尔在当时刚刚起步的工业光魔公司开始了他的特效职业生涯，在《帝国反击战》中担任设备设计师和摄像助理，然后成为《绝地归来》中的摄像师。尼尔随后前往 Boss Film 工作，并于 1983 年首次见到马克·斯泰森 (Mark Stetson)，十年后加入数字王国。尼尔过去曾与要求严格的导演合作过，但贝松坚持保留他所有的创意选择——这一警告可能会给视觉效果工作带来麻烦。 “我们拍摄了许多备用背景板——不同的角度——只有在编辑阶段我们才能看到什么是可行的，”尼尔最近在伦敦的一次电话交谈中回忆道，他即将在伦敦开始担任即将上映的詹姆斯·邦德电影《明日帝国》的视觉效果总监。 “我没有摄制组，所以吕克和蒂埃里拍摄了所有的底片，我在技术上帮助他们解决后期问题。”贝松为《第五元素》设想了一种非常对称、中心重点的电影风格。虽然导演之前的电影都是采用 2.35:1 变形宽银幕拍摄的，但《第五元素》将采用 Super 35 拍摄，部分原因是为了满足强烈的视觉效果需求。这一决定意味着制作团队不必拍摄 VistaVision 背景板或使用重复的摄影机包。主单元使用 Arriflex 摄像机，Stetson、Neil 和公司不情愿地同意使用 Arris 进行特效工作。 “我们对此表示怀疑，”尼尔承认。 “我们试图推动 Panavision，因为我们觉得我们更有机会拥有稳定的平板摄影机，但 Arris 相当不错。制作使用的是 Arri 535B，我们的大部分底片都是用量产摄影机拍摄的。我们还使用了新型 435 高速摄影机的原型。大约在制作进行到一半时，Arriflex 用他们的第一款量产 435 摄影机替换了我们的原型。我对这款摄影机在稳定性方面的表现感到惊讶。事实上，它在任何速度下都非常稳定，足以胜任从每秒 2 帧到 150 帧的遮罩工作——无论是前进还是后退，我从未见过世界上任何地方制造的相机能够做到这一点。”虽然 Arris 证明了自己的价值，但特效团队对一些摄影师因稳定性问题而拒绝拍摄柯达欧洲成品库存的故事感到担忧，并说服制作团队进口罗彻斯特成品乳剂以用于任何涉及视觉效果的内容以及周围的镜头。最终，阿博加斯特使用柯达 5293 拍摄非效果序列，并在速度较慢的 5248 上拍摄了影片的大量绿屏工作。虽然大多数效果板内部都是在 5293 年拍摄的，但尼尔在 5245 年的“准魔法时刻”拍摄了一些作为寺庙的“垂直岩石块”的板片。尽管毛里塔尼亚板是在正式设计效果镜头之前拍摄的，但斯泰森说，该系列产生了“六个宇宙飞船镜头的漂亮序列。”8 英尺的 Mondoshawan 宇宙飞船微缩模型是由尼尔的视觉效果拍摄的联合摄影师保罗·金特里 (Paul Gentry) 将模型灯光与画面上的玫瑰色日落相匹配，蒙多沙万人参观了寺庙，然后再次升空。根据电影的故事情节，公元 2259 年，地球发现自己陷入了危机。当蒙多沙万人的母舰在外星球上被可怕的曼格洛人击落时，他们的任务是将所有五种元素带到地球（由尼克出色的外星人设计实现）。正如 Besson 和 Stetson 所设想的那样，他们的小型 ZFX 200 战斗机在巨大的 Mondoshawan 飞船上进行动态俯冲，用炽热的导弹扫射其表面。“我们首先在 Mondoshawan 飞船上进行了移动，然后在小型 ZFX 200 战斗机上进行了不同的动作，”Stetson 回忆道，“我们为微型攻击机准备了两种尺寸：一个长约 16 英寸的清理设计模型。镜头直接跟随被毁坏的飞船撞向行星表面，外壳在撞击时破裂，让人回想起兴登堡号的毁灭。“我认为马克是根据他在航展上看到的一场实际坠机事件拍摄的，当时一架相当大的喷气式飞机坠毁在地面上，”尼尔说。鼻子到尾巴。这就是指导原则：这个东西在船的直径之外钻入并破裂，船的宽度覆盖了撞击点。”蒙多沙万号船的“毁灭”是在摄影棚中拍摄的。“我们将整个环境旋转了 90 度，所以我们在船尾的舞台上看，并将我们的运动控制摄像机拉回来，”尼尔说。“我们创建了交互式照明效果，如火光，作为一种甜味剂，帮助将模型加入到“在创建该序列的过程中，没有任何模特受到伤害，”斯泰森笑着补充道。“这是一种哲学问题。我正在处理一部太空奇幻电影，但我不想再拍一部大型“微型爆炸”电影。我不想建造一艘巨大的 40 英尺长的宇宙飞船，然后将其扔到行星表面并爆炸；我想对实际爆炸做一些更猛烈的事情，然后将船缩放到其中。 Scott Rader 跟踪了微缩模型的大量表面爆炸——其中一些是我们拍摄的，一些来自 Digital Domain 火炮库。然后，我们跟随蒙多沙万号飞船撞上红色星球，这是一个典型的“卢克视觉”镜头，当它撞击表面时，从船尾直视。造成的大规模爆炸涉及印第安沙丘的巨大火焰射击。 “这颗行星是一块约 50 英尺宽、125 英尺深的地面，经过精心雕刻，布满了小陨石坑，”尼尔报告道。 “我们选择那个地点是因为它给了我们下午晚些时候的光线，而且相对平坦；我们可以在不引发草火的情况下进行烟火活动。塞恩·莫里斯和他的同事伊利亚·波波夫组装了一个由 45 到 50 门迫击炮组成的巨大系统，在景观中心的一个环中设置，这样当船撞上它们时，它们都会从中心向外喷射。他们还在外面设置了底漆绳，以产生碎片喷雾。这是一个非常巧妙设计的烟火活动。我们并排放置了两台小型高速 Photosonics 4ML 摄像机，并限制了帧速率，因为与布置布景的成本相比，额外的摄像机成本很小。我们拍摄了两次正式镜头，帧速率约为 100 fps，因为天色较晚，而且我想保持烟火的深度。”除了创造风格化但可信的太空战斗之外，数字王国的工作人员还必须为贝松的奇妙故事设想邪恶（大写的E）。当一艘巨大的战舰遇到一颗发光的行星大小的熔岩球时，观众第一次看到了邪恶。为了应对它的潜在威胁，一队巡洋舰方阵向它发射了三枚导弹。然而，这种策略只会让这个巨大的球体变得更大——也更愤怒。当战舰愚蠢地向邪恶发射更多导弹时，球体喷出一团火球，吞噬了战舰。数字王国为其中一艘地球战舰添加了更多细节。战舰对抗邪恶之球，这是由一群 CG 艺术家在数字效果总监 Karen Goulekas 的监督下使用 Alias 软件实现的。邪恶效果是通过 Renderman、Prisms、Alias 和专有软件实现的，由一组由数字效果总监 Karen Goulekas 监督的 CG 艺术家操作。 “我们花了很长时间来研究地壳和熔岩的外观，以及它们相遇时发生的活动，”古莱卡斯说。 “我们开发了一个三步过程来创建表面外观，大大减少了渲染时间。首先，Paul Van Camp 编写了一个分形生成器来创建分形动画纹理以及熔岩和地壳的位移图。然后，在 Elastic Reality（一个 2D 变形程序）中的这些贴图之上添加了额外的扭曲和膨胀。最后，将生成的贴图输入 Renderman 着色器，以根据高度场将地壳和熔岩及其伴随的位移应用到球体上，这将六小时的渲染时间压缩为每帧半小时。”邪恶通常看起来是一个看似简单的存在，但古莱卡斯发现这种无定形异常的动画非常复杂：“控制熔岩和地壳并不容易，因为卢克希望邪恶做一些非常具体的事情：当它闭合时，它必须结壳并变黑，之后地壳必须旋转。最困难的事情是当导弹射向它时让邪恶生气并成长。卢克不希望它只是扩大规模，这会看起来就像我们在放大一个球体，而我们也没有使用数学表达式，保罗·范坎普想出了一种以非常非线性的方式扩展它的方法，因此球变得更加无定形，感觉就像它实际上在增长一样。”邪恶变得愤怒。随着邪恶的入侵，贝松在整部电影中偶尔回到外太空。影片的主角——出租车司机科本·达拉斯（布鲁斯·威利斯饰）和神秘迷人的莉露（米拉·乔沃维奇饰）在地球上相遇后，他们乘坐航天飞机离开纽约市，并与一艘悬停在田园诗般的水星球表面上的壮观的 2,000 英尺长的游船（弗洛斯顿天堂号）会合。他们的任务：在这艘未来派的爱情船上与外星歌剧女主角会合。这段精彩片段从航天飞机与轨道游轮对接开始，游轮上涂有华丽的油漆，斯泰森称之为“糖果苹果蓝”。当然，油漆的反光特性使得 8 英尺的微型模型很难拍摄。与第五元素的所有模型一样，比尔·尼尔 (Bill Neil) 使用 Digital Domain 在阿波罗 13 号上首创的背光 UV 技术拍摄了《Fhloston Paradise》。然后拍摄逆光通道，将涂有紫外线敏感涂料的屏幕放置在模型后面。当用 Kino Flo 击中时，屏幕会发出带宽频率非常窄的红色荧光，使模型看起来像红色衬托下的黑色剪影。背光图像仅记录在胶片的红色层上，形成完美的哑光效果，同时允许大量的反射光延伸到模型表面之外——这是使用传统绿屏技术会失去的额外触感。 Fhlostin Paradise 悬浮在一个田园诗般的水星球表面上空，该星球是使用 Arete（与 Waterworld 中使用的软件相同）创建的。这艘六英尺微型船的油漆工作具有反光特性，因此拍摄起来很困难。与第五元素的所有模型一样，比尔·尼尔使用 Digital Domain 在阿波罗 13 号上首创的背光紫外线技术来拍摄飞船。尼尔担心拍摄时这艘深蓝色的飞船会呈现出怎样的效果。他回忆道，“它最初非常闪亮，所以我们在光泽和半哑光之间找到了一个很好的平衡，它很好地吸收了光线，并且仍然有一些光泽。我做了一些照明测试，将微型模型楔入以观察它在光线下的表现，发现通过显着过度曝光，它会变得越来越丰富。很奇怪；油漆本身实际上会发光。我反复调整曝光，直到找到了我认为适合热带环境的外观。”为了与女主角序列的色彩主题保持一致，一个令人惊叹的蓝色外星人，头上伸出六根触手，在游轮上拥挤的音乐厅前唱歌。女主角越过肩膀看向人群的角度实际上是在伦敦考文特花园皇家歌剧院的外景拍摄的，但相反的角度是在松林工作室的舞台上的绿屏上拍摄的。绿幕镜头原本应该是锁定的，但当斯泰森、古勒卡斯和尼尔到达片场时，他们发现贝松再次设置了移动车轨道。负责编码设备的迈克·伯格斯特罗姆 (Mike Bergstrom) 迅速将生产摄像机上的编码头更换为重复的编码头。古莱卡斯在绿屏上贴满了乒乓球，用于跟踪参考点。与此同时，斯泰森担心女主角服装的反光光泽。 “[化妆师]尼克·杜德曼使用了一种非常镜面反射和衍射的材料，”斯泰森说。 “当我们看到身穿闪亮蓝色套装的女主角的绿屏元素时，我们想，‘天啊，我们能从中得到什么？’”幸运的是，尼尔一直忙着为他的第一组同事阿博加斯特提供建议，以确保特效团队得到坚实的绿屏支持。 “我说服蒂埃里调整他的灯光，以提高从这件半金属服装上拉出遮罩的可能性”尼尔回忆道。 “他在舞台上添加了一些侧光；他最初并没有计划这样做，但这帮助我们控制了服装中的绿色溢出。”
  4. 当 Stetson 和 Goulekas 回到 Digital Domain 时，乐趣才真正开始，整个背景——包括一个巨大的画窗，由巨大的钢拱门框架，提供了漂浮在太空中的 Fhloston 行星的视图——被创建为二维数字哑光绘画。蓝色的水星球本身是一个 CG 创作，其天空和云彩由艺术总监 Ron Gress 用数字绘制而成。虽然 Besson 和 Arbogast 从移动轨道上拍摄了这位女主角的表演，但 Goulekas 报告说，“Luc 几乎实现了锁定，其中有一点相机漂移，所以我们认为我们不必使用编码数据，我们只需采用传统的 2-D，跟踪乒乓标记以获得我们的 X 和 Y 平移曲线，然后将它们应用到我们的 CG 背景中。”尽管排列 2D 遮罩看起来很容易，但 Goulekas 的团队仍在努力通过眼睛为舞台和拱门创建正确的视角。在一个突破性的概念中，Goulekas 和合成主管 Jonathan Egstad 将编码的多莉移动导入数字王国的专有合成软件 Nuke 中。 “突然之间，所有的倾斜、平移和元素看起来都正确了，”古莱卡斯惊叹道。 “拱门的哑光绘画上实际上有 3D 倾斜，拱门投射到舞台上的阴影经过数学计算，可以随着摄像机的透视移动。我对此感到非常自豪；它们确实涉及到很多三维思维和内置摄像机透视。”类似的技术帮助《第五元素》将微缩模型、CG 模型和数字哑光绘画最壮观的融合在一起，在一场令人惊叹的警察追捕中，穿越了未来派的纽约市，老城街道上方 200 层，下方 400 层。警车和他们的猎物，一辆属于科本达拉斯的破旧出租车，都在空中——现场的所有其他汽车也是如此。为运动控制拍摄而设置的微型驾驶室。科本的驾驶室喷气式飞机高高地飞过纽约市的多飞机交通流。创建这些飞行器中队的复杂性使 Karen Goulekas 确信需要设计一个程序管道，使许多艺术家能够产生流量，并在她的团队为 Besson 做的预可视化中使用相同的软件。因此，从理论上讲，准备的摄像机移动随后可以转化为运动控制阶段，并用于将微缩模型、CG 模型和绘景混合在一起。 “在伦敦，我们使用 Silicon Graphics 的 Prisms 软件进行预览，”Goulekas 说。 “这是我们在数字王国有史以来的第一个大型视觉预览，所以最初倾向于使用更便宜的软件包来完成它。但我真的很犹豫这个想法。马克希望视觉预览能够直接投入生产，而不是在另一个软件包上重新创建真实的视觉预览。这样，我们就可以将整个序列与 CG 中的平面阴影城市剪辑在一起；当我们回到数字王国时，我们可以将这些磁带发送到舞台，这样工作人员不仅可以有相机曲线，还有镜头应该是什么样子的视觉动画。”除了确定摄像机移动和拍摄角度之外，棱镜预可视化还帮助 Stetson 和模型车间主管 Neils Neilson 确定微型建筑的范围。坎宁安设置了该软件，这样他就可以改变建筑物的比例，而无需重新激活每个镜头，这使得斯泰森能够尝试各种比例，直到找到与数字王国运动控制舞台的高度和宽度完美匹配的方案。 “我们还对舞台环境进行了建模，”Stetson 解释道，“这样我们就可以了解我们的微型城市景观是否适合 Digital Domain 的舞台。然后我们在预览中对建筑物进行颜色编码，这样我们就知道何时超出了舞台的限制。” “这是一个边界框，”古莱卡斯补充道。 “当我们离开摄像机 30 或 40 英尺并到达舞台地板时，渲染出来的所有东西都变成红色，所以我们自动知道什么是 CG 或绘景或微型建筑。”斯泰森将图纸和蓝图来回传递给特效艺术总监罗恩·格雷斯 (Ron Gress) 和艾拉·吉尔福德 (Ira Gilford)，以设计城市景观并进入数字王国的模型店，从而在斯泰森从英国返回之前开始施工。这是一个压力很大的过程，因为贝松和制作设计师丹·韦尔坚持对纽约市的复古愿景，即使它向上爬了数百层楼，也能保持街道的严格秩序。有些结构必须看起来像是建在旧建筑物的顶部，这些旧建筑物将在强化玻璃箱下形成方形。 “丹·韦尔最早对我说的一句话是，‘法国人看着曼哈顿，看到的城市结构与古典欧洲城市完全不同。’与巴黎或伦敦不同……纽约没有曲线或丁字路口——一切都与网格有关。” - 标记斯泰森“卢克和丹对我们城市的看法与我以前合作过的任何导演和美术设计师都不同，”斯泰森说，“我可能已经建造了六次纽约！丹韦尔对我最早的说法之一是，‘一个法国人看着曼哈顿，看到了一个与古典欧洲城市完全不同的城市结构。’”与巴黎或伦敦的小街道老鼠巢不同，纽约没有弯道或丁字路口——一切都是网格状的。纽约拥有无限的视角，这与吕克·贝松在这部电影中的电影摄影风格非常吻合——以中心为中心，在十字线处有单点视角和消失点。”尽管事实证明创造看似永远延伸的街道很困难，但斯泰森最终还是选择了 1/24 比例的 Element 纽约市（与他在《哈德吸克代理》和《影子》中使用的比例相同）。 “1/24 的比例大约是现代相机所能达到的最小比例，”他说。 “我们可能有 24 到 30 座微型建筑，它们相当大——高达 24 英尺高，40 英尺深，填满了数字王国的主双层舞台。我们有一些镜头完全是微缩背景，但大多数时候我们最终都用 2D 哑光绘画非常有创意地扩展了场景。大约有 5 个镜头，其中城市景观完全由 CG 呈现。但在城市的外部场景方面，很少有真人制作的表现。几乎完全留给我们了。”斯泰森、古莱卡斯和尼尔仍在英国为莉露的逃亡拍摄几幅全尺寸布景（在影片中，她首先看到了巨大的纽约大都市，然后落入科本的出租车并带领警察在时代广场上下进行追捕），数字王国的模型店将微缩模型搬上了舞台。 “我们回来一个月后，我们开始拍摄模特，”斯泰森回忆道。 “在这个设施中，Karen 第一次能够真正将摄像机动作导出到舞台。它不是一条单行道（记录运动控制动作然后发送到 CG 世界），而是实际上将预视摄像机动作放入运动控制文件中。有时，效果如广告所示，有时效果较差。下次，我们将制作更准确的运动控制轴 CG 模型，以便可以更准确地模仿摄像机的移动和限制。不过，我们创造了这条路径，我们为此感到自豪。完成后我们互相看着对方说，‘我们真的成功了！’”预可视化中的 3D 空间并不总是与物理舞台相关，但特效电影摄影师 Bill Neil 和他的同事 Paul Gentry 发现它很有用，即使只是作为时间指南。尼尔解释说：“这是视觉效果团队和导演之间的一个极好的沟通工具，可以帮助澄清他的愿景所包含的内容，并达成共识。”尼尔坚持在拍摄汽车之前先拍摄城市背景板，以便在车辆上创建与其环境相关的互动灯光。尽管贝松的中心聚焦视角与尼尔的构图经验相反，但摄影师发现它提供了一些有趣的问题需要解决——例如在拍摄通过数字哑光绘画将有限数量的建筑物拉长到无限时创造深度。贝松要求这一场景发生在光天化日之下，这让这一挑战雪上加霜。 “我很震惊，”尼尔承认。 “微缩模型往往因为你看不到太多而得以保存，但这整个序列是在当天晚些时候进行的，并且必须经得起仔细审查。舞台天花板仅距建筑物顶部几英尺，因此我们进行了测试，看看如何照亮那片广阔的远景。幸运的是，我的灯光师乔治·鲍尔和我的关键人物乔·塞莱斯特能够在舞台上实现我的想法。我们有一个来自顶部，然后是金色的主光，应该是傍晚的阳光穿过建筑物之间的任何间隙，但我没有让主光全部来自大致相同的方向，而是采用了所谓的“断裂光方法”，其中我添加了来自不在框架内的建筑物的断裂光，通常以陡峭的角度从阳光主光反射回来，这给了城市一种能量，一种非常美妙的光调制。距相机的距离增加，场景的对比度降低。深色的东西变得更亮，浅色的东西变得更暗，直到最终变得单调。我在微型灯光中考虑到了这一点，并在哑光绘画中体现了这一点，这给了我们街道上很大的深度。”斯泰森为击落建筑物峡谷所需的运动控制装置设定了一些基本规格：“我想让飞机飞行“我们将摄像机从舞台天花板向下投射到微缩模型中，因此我们将摄像机安装在十字形运动控制装置上，这是一种运动控制起重机，带有用于下降的垂直塔和用于向前运动的水平横梁。”Stetson 还计划将摄像机安装在 20 英尺长的延伸臂上穿过微缩模型街道。四英尺宽的街道太窄，尼尔的运动控制摄像机无法沿着建筑物之间的水平轨道运行。对于追逐的高潮，当 Korben 的驾驶室在地下铁路下方的梁墙隧道中被追踪，斯泰森希望尼尔能够使用延伸臂进入 1/6 比例的隧道模型，尽管微型布景长度为 48 英尺，但对于高速追逐来说，它仍然不够长。“我们通过拍摄两次，第二次将摄像机向后移动 48 英尺，使其看起来长了一倍，”斯泰森说，“我们需要一个很长的摄像机轨道和两组遮罩。完成了它，但在凯伦的帮助下，这些碎片完美地排列在一起。隧道追逐几乎完全是微型的。 Brian Grill 加入了一些 CG 碎片、一些 2D 碎片，以及从之前的节目中复活的烟雾，让场景变得更加甜美。出租车在前景，消失点在中间，有大量的摇摆和摆动。”在 5 分钟和 70 多个镜头中，出租车追逐序列代表了数字王国第五元素效果的近三分之一。大多数镜头涉及 CG 交通，有时还有 CG 英雄出租车和警车。“我们将 Korben 的出租车和警车的 1/6 比例[模型]数字化，”Goulekas说：“英雄警车在 Leeloo 跳跃后俯冲而下是在 CG 中完成的，因为我们想看到所有六个表面；没有地方可以隐藏电枢安装座。你会感到惊讶的。我们的 CG 交通管道使我们能够说，“我们想要一定比例的出租车、警车、红色汽车、蓝色汽车。”艺术家使用低分辨率版本的交通，然后我们在 Tenderer 的输出上替换真实的交通。
  5. 序列主管 Sean Cunningham 协调了出租车追逐的所有片段的创建，着色器主管 Simon O' Connor 构思了交通的所有着色器和表面。但将所有不同元素组合在一起的任务就落在了合成主管 Egstad 的身上。 “在白天使所有物体的色彩平衡和对比度范围正确匹配是一个巨大的挑战，”埃格斯塔德说。 “我们合成了数百辆计算机图形汽车，除非我们稍微模糊它们并提高它们的对比度，否则它们是不适合的。此外，哑光边缘几乎没有那么宽容，因此我们使用了许多不同的大气技巧，例如添加密度雾并在 Flame 中使用滤镜来添加来自车头灯的漂亮光学发光并消除 CG 对象的诅咒。”在疯狂的制作过程中，斯泰森只能依靠自己的艺术本能来指导城市景观的发展——这是一种潜在的危险情况。 “拍摄时，我们会把卢克送上舞台，但他只是看着东西，没有任何反应，因为他完全专注于指导演员，”斯泰森回忆道。 “如果我真的错误地理解了 Luc 想要的东西，我们就会在时间和金钱方面付出巨大的代价。“最后，当我们在设计方面已经走到了无法回头的地步时，我去了他位于马里布的编辑室，向他展示了该城市第一个大型模型设置之一的测试镜头的彩色打印，这代表了价值数十万美元的工作。到那时，还不知道他是否会祝福。卢克一看，脸色顿时亮了起来。他举起照片让编辑西尔维·兰德拉指着它，然后指着他脸上挂着灿烂笑容的脸。他真的很喜欢它。这是一个巨大的解脱。”

Original Summary

  1. If the box office smash of the Star Wars Special Editions proves anything, it's that audiences are hungry again for what purists once disparagingly referred to as "a space opera." However, French director Luc Besson's audacious new film, The Fifth Element, owes less to George Lucas than to the absurdist sensibilities of Jean-Luc Godard. Likewise, the film's intense imagery was dreamed up in part by French artists Jean "Moebius" Giraud (of the Gallic graphic magazine Metal Hurlant) and Jean-Claude Mezieres (Valérian: Spatio-Temporal Agent), who, along with production designer Dan Weil, helped Besson visualize a fantasy film which the director had nurtured for nearly 20 years. Along the way, the filmmaker refined and expanded his ideas to include every effects innovation of the past two decades.
  2. This article originally appeared in AC May 1997. Some photos are additional or alternate. The result is a visually stunning, living comic book that plays out across a fully realized galaxy — from a New York stretching 600 stories in every direction (complete with traffic jams caused by flying cars) to a luxurious outerspace pleasure ship cruising over a water planet. The Fifth Element's far-reaching vision of the future demanded a virtual "sampler" of visual effects, and Besson chose Digital Domain to achieve it all. Approximately 85 model makers and 85 artists worked on finishing the film's 220-plus effects shots at any one time. Leading the pack was first-time visual effects supervisor Mark Stetson. A former model maker (Blade Runner, The Hudsucker Proxy), Stetson closed his shop, Stetson Visual Services, just after completing a colossal miniature of the ill-fated Exxon Valdez for Waterworld. Within four months, he joined Digital Domain on The Fifth Element. Assessing the duo's on-set relationship, visual effects producer Dan Lombardo (The Island of Dr. Moreau) notes, "Luc is the type of director who is very hands-on with every aspect of a production. On this show, he didn't delegate a whole lot, except when it came to something he didn't know very well. That's when he'd lean over to Mark and ask him to step in." Stetson himself affirms, "It was great to have that kind of relationship with Luc, who is normally extremely private and protective. The movie's design was heavily influenced by the look of Seventies' French comic books, which made it really fun to work on. Although part of it takes place in a futuristic New York City, I think the setting is quite different from the Los Angeles of Blade Runner. The flying cars are a lot more whimsical, and the city is set in broad daylight. The film is rooted in a much more utopian vision of the future than Blade Runner, which virtually defined the post-apocalyptic look of futuristic films for more than a decade." The Fifth Element opens in early 20th-century Egypt as a spaceship — piloted by a saintly race of aliens called the Mondoshawan — descends toward Earth. A pleasing, rusty red hybrid of a conch shell and a football, the alien craft was designed by Metal Hurlant artist Moebius, and was constructed as an 8’ miniature by Digital Domain's model shop; a digital matte painting derived from NASA photographs doubled for Earth. The football-shaped Mondoshawan craft is put through its paces. Upon arriving, the oval Mondoshawan craft hovers on energy beams above a bizarre rock temple. The production crew painted a doorway on a gigantic rock rising from a dry lake bed, transforming it into the temple exterior. Most of the plates were shot by Besson and Fifth Element cinematographer Thierry Arbogast, AFC, with Digital Domain's visual effects director of photography, Bill Neil, acting as an advisor.
  3. After a decade working as a camera assistant and camera operator, Neil began his effects career at the then-fledgling Industrial Light & Magic as an equipment designer and camera assistant on The Empire Strikes Back and then became a camera operator on Return of the Jedi. Neil subsequently went to work at Boss Film, where he first met Mark Stetson in 1983, before joining Digital Domain a decade later. Neil had worked with demanding directors in the past, but Besson insisted that all of his creative options be left open — a caveat which could have spelled trouble for visual effects work. "We shot a number of alternate background plates — different angles — and it was only in the editing phase that we saw what was going to work," Neil recalled during a recent phone conversation from London, where he was about to begin work as visual effects supervisor on the upcoming James Bond film Tomorrow Never Dies. "I didn't have a camera crew, so Luc and Thierry photographed all the plates, and I helped them technically with post issues." Besson had a very symmetrical, center-weighted cinematographic style in mind for The Fifth Element. While the director had shot his previous films in 2.35:1 anamorphic, The Fifth Element would be shot in Super 35, partly to accommodate the intense visual effects demands. This decision meant that the production didn't have to shoot VistaVision background plates or use duplicate camera packages. The main unit used Arriflex cameras, and Stetson, Neil and company reluctantly agreed to use the Arris for their effects work. "We were skeptical," Neil admits. "We tried to push toward Panavision because we felt we had a better chance of having a steady plate camera, but the Arris were pretty good. The production was using the Arri 535B, and most of our plates were shot with a production camera. We also used a prototype of the new 435 high-speed camera. About halfway through production, Arriflex replaced our prototype with one of their first production 435 cameras. I was amazed at the performance of this camera in terms of its steadiness at all speeds. In fact, it was rock steady, good enough for matte work from two frames to 150 frames a second — in both forward and reverse. I've never seen any camera made anyplace in the world that could do that." While the Arris proved their worth, the effects team was concerned about stories of cameramen who refused to shoot Kodak's European-finished stocks because of steadiness issues, and convinced the production to import Rochester-finished emulsions for anything that was to involve visual effects, as well as the surrounding footage. Ultimately, Arbogast used Kodak 5293 for non-effects sequences, and shot the picture's considerable amount of greenscreen work on the slower 5248 stock. While most effects plate interiors were shot on 5293, Neil shot some plates of the "vertical hunk of rock" that served as the temple on 5245 at "quasi-magic hour. Though the Mauritania plates were filmed prior to a formal design of the effects shots, Stetson says that the series yielded "a nice sequence of six spaceship shots." The 8’ Mondoshawan spaceship miniature was shot by Neil's visual effects co-cinematographer, Paul Gentry, who matched the model lighting to the plate's rosy sunset look as the Mondoshawan visit the temple and then lift off again. According to the film's storyline, the Earth finds itself in crisis in the year 2259 A.D. The Mondoshawan are on a mission to bring all five elements to Earth when their mothership is shot down over an alien planet by the dreaded Mangalores (realized with excellent alien designs by Nick Dudman). As envisioned by Besson and Stetson, their tiny ZFX 200 fighters make a dynamic dive on the massive Mondoshawan craft, raking its surface with fiery missiles. "We shot the move on the Mondoshawan ship first, then did different moves on the little ZFX 200 fighters," Stetson remembers. "We had two scales for the miniature attackers: a cleaned-up design maquette measuring about 16" long was used for long shots, and a 4' miniature for close-ups. The camera follows directly behind the devastated craft as it crashes into the planet's surface. Flames erupt through its shell, which crumples on impact, recalling the destruction of the Hindenburg. "I think Mark patterned it after an actual crash he saw on film where a pretty sizable jet plane crashed into the ground at an airshow," Neil says. "It went in at high speed and disintegrated little by little from the nose to the tail. That was the guiding principle: this thing was boring in and breaking up beyond the diameter of the ship, whose width covers the impact point." The "destruction" of the Mondoshawan ship was shot on a soundstage. "We turned the whole environment 90 degrees, so we were looking across the stage at the tail of the ship and pulling our motion-control camera back," Neil says. "We created interactive lighting effects, like firelight, as a sweetener to help join the model to the pyrotechnic plate." "No models were harmed during the creation of the sequence," Stetson adds with a laugh. "That was sort of a philosophical thing. I was dealing with a space fantasy here, but I didn't want to do another big 'miniature explosion' movie. I didn't want to build a huge 40’ spaceship, drop it into a planet surface and explode it; I wanted to do something a little more violent with the actual explosion and then just scale the ship into it. Scott Rader tracked in a lot of surface explosions to the miniature — some that we had photographed and some from the Digital Domain pyro library. We then followed the Mondoshawan ship as it crashed into the red planet, which was a typical 'Luc-vision' shot looking straight down the tail of the ship as it impacted the surface. The massive explosion that results involved a huge pyro shoot at Indian Dunes. "The planet was a patch of ground about 50' wide by 125' deep, which was sculpted very carefully with little craters," Neil reports. "We chose that spot because it gave us late afternoon light and was relatively flat; we could do pyrotechnic events without starting grass fires. Thaine Morris and his associate, Ilya Popov, assembled a huge system of 45 to 50 mortars set up in a ring in the center of the landscape so they'd all blow from the center out as the ship hit. They also set up primer cord around the outside to create a spray of debris. It was a very cleverly choreographed pyrotechnic event. We put two small, high-speed Photosonics 4ML cameras side by side and bracketed the frame rate because additional camera costs were small compared to the cost of dressing the set. We did two formal takes. The final frame-rate was around 1OO fps. We shot on 5293 because it was fairly dark late in the day, and I wanted to hold the depth of the pyrotechnics." Aside from creating stylized but believable space battles, Digital Domain's crew had to envision Evil (with a capital E) for Besson's fantastic tale. Audiences first glimpse Evil when a massive warship encounters a glowing, planet-sized ball of lava. Responding to its potential menace, a phalanx of cruisers fire three missiles at it. This tactic, however, only serves to make the vast orb larger — and angrier. When the warships foolishly fire more missiles into Evil, the sphere spits out a ball of flame that engulfs the craft. Additional detail is added to one of Earth's warships at Digital Domain. Warships confront the ball of Evil, which was realized with Alias software manipulated by a crew of CG artists overseen by digital effects supervisor Karen Goulekas. The Evil effect was realized with Renderman, Prisms, Alias and proprietary software manipulated by a crew of CG artists overseen by digital effects supervisor Karen Goulekas. "We worked for a long time to develop the look of the crust and lava, and the activity that occurs when they meet," Goulekas says. "We developed a three-step process to create the look of the surface that greatly reduced rendering time. First, Paul Van Camp wrote a fractal generator to create fractal-animated texture and displacement maps of lava and crust. Then, additional twisting and bulging was added on top of these maps in Elastic Reality, a 2-D morphing program. Finally, the resulting maps were fed into a Renderman Shader to apply the crust and lava and their accompanying displacement on a sphere based on height fields, which condensed six hours of render time to a half hour per frame." Evil often appears to be a deceptively simple presence, but Goulekas found the amorphous anomaly terribly complex to animate: "It wasn't easy to control the lava and the crust, because Luc wanted Evil to do some really specific things: when it closed up, it had to crust over and turn black, after which the crust had to rotate. The hardest thing was making Evil get angry and grow when the missiles were shot at it. Luc didn't want it to just scale up, which would have looked as if we were zooming in on a sphere, and neither did we. Using math expressions, Paul Van Camp came up with a way to expand it in a very non-linear fashion so the ball became more amorphous and felt as if it were actually growing." Evil grows angry. As Evil encroaches, Besson returns to outer space sporadically throughout the film. After the film's protagonists — cab driver Korben Dallas (Bruce Willis) and the cryptically beguiling Leeloo (Milla Jovovich) — meet on Earth, they take a space shuttle out of New York City and rendezvous with a spectacular 2,000’ pleasure ship (the Fhloston Paradise) hovering above the surface of an idyllic water planet. Their mission: rendezvous with an alien opera diva aboard this futuristic Love Boat. The diva sequence begins as the space shuttle docks with the orbiting cruise ship, which sports a garish paint job in a hue that Stetson terms as "candy apple blue." Naturally, the reflective nature of the paint made the 8’ miniature difficult to shoot. As with all of The Fifth Element's models, Bill Neil shot the Fhloston Paradise using the backlit UV technique Digital Domain pioneered on Apollo 13. The frontlit beauty, key and fill passes were filmed against black; a backlight pass was then shot, with a screen painted with UV-sensitive paint positioned behind the model. When hit with a Kino Flo, the screen fluoresces a very narrow bandwidth frequency of red, making the model appear as a black silhouette against red. The backlit image only registers on the red layer of the film, creating a perfect matte while allowing a fair amount of reflective glow to extend beyond the model's surface — an extra touch that would have been lost using traditional greenscreen techniques. The Fhlostin Paradise hovers above the surface of an idyllic water planet created using Arete, the same software employed on Waterworld. The reflective nature of the six-foot miniature ship's paint job made it difficult to shoot. As with all of The Fifth Element's models, Bill Neil shot the craft using the backlit UV technique Digital Domain pioneered on Apollo 13. Neil had concerns about how the intensely blue spacecraft would appear when photographed. He recalls, "It was quite shiny initially, so we found a good balance between gloss and semimatte, where it picked up the light nicely and still had some sheen to it. I did some lighting tests, wedging the miniature to see how it behaved in the light, and found that by overexposing it significantly, it just got richer and richer. It was strange; the paint itself actually glowed. I played around with the exposure until I found a look I thought was suitable to a tropical environment." In keeping with the diva sequence's color motif, a stunning blue alien, replete with six tentacles protruding from her head, sings before a packed concert hall aboard the cruise ship. Angles looking over the diva's shoulder at the crowd were actually shot on location at London's Covent Garden Royal Opera House, but the reverse angles were shot against green screen on a stage at Pinewood Studios. The greenscreen shots were originally supposed to be lockoffs, but when Stetson, Goulekas and Neil arrived on the set, they found that Besson had once again set up dolly tracks. Mike Bergstrom, who handled the encoding equipment, quickly swapped the head on the production camera with a duplicate encoding head. Goulekas plastered the greenscreen with ping-pong balls for tracking reference points. Meanwhile, Stetson was concerned about the reflective sheen of the diva's costume. "[Makeup artist] Nick Dudman used a material that was very specular and diffractive," Stetson says. "When we looked at the green screen element of the diva in this shiny blue suit, we thought, 'Oh God, what are we going to get out of this?'" Fortunately, Neil had been busily advising his first-unit colleague Arbogast to ensure that the effects team received a solid green screen backing. "I convinced Thierry to adjust his lighting in order to improve the possibilities of pulling mattes off this semi-metallic costume" Neil recalls. "He added some sidelight to the stage; he hadn't originally planned to do that, but it helped us to control the green spill from the costume."
  4. The fun really began once Stetson and Goulekas returned to Digital Domain, where the entire background — including a huge picture window framed by giant steel arches, offering a view of the planet Fhloston floating in space — was created as a 2-D digital matte painting. The blue water planet itself was a CG creation, with digitally painted skies and clouds created by art director Ron Gress. Although Besson and Arbogast shot the diva's performance from the dolly track, Goulekas reports that "Luc pretty much delivered lock-offs with a little bit of camera drift in them, so we figured we didn't have to go with the encoded data, we'd just go traditional 2-D, track the ping-pong markers to get our X and Y translation curves, and then apply them to our CG background." Despite the apparent ease of lining up the 2-D matte, Goulekas' team was sweating to create the correct perspective for the stage and arches by eye. In a breakthrough concept, Goulekas and compositing supervisor Jonathan Egstad imported the encoded dolly move into Nuke, Digital Domain's proprietary compositing software. "Suddenly, all of the tilts, pans and elements looked correct," Goulekas marvels. "There's actually a 3-D tilt on the matte painting of the arches, and the shadows cast by the arches onto the stage were mathematically worked out to move in perspective along with the camera. I'm pretty proud of those; they really involved a lot of three-dimensional thought and built-in camera perspective." A similar technique helped tie The Fifth Element's most spectacular blend of miniatures, CG models and digital matte paintings together in an awe-inspiring police chase through a futuristic New York City that extends 200 stories above the old city streets and 400 stories below. Both the police cars and their quarry, a beatup cab belonging to Korben Dallas, are airborne — as are all of the other cars in the scene. The miniature cab set up for a motion-control shot. Korben's cab jets high above NYC's multi-plane traffic streams. The complexity of creating these squadrons of flying vehicles convinced Karen Goulekas of the need to devise a procedural pipeline that would enable many artists to generate traffic, and to employ the same software in the pre-visualizations her team was doing for Besson. Thus, in theory, the preparatory camera moves could later be translated to the motion-control stage and employed to blend the miniatures, CG models and matte paintings together. "In London, we used Silicon Graphics' Prisms software for the pre-vis," Goulekas says. "It was our first big pre-vis ever at Digital Domain, so initially there was an inclination to do it with a less expensive software package. But I really balked at that idea. Rather than re-creating the pre-vis for real on another software package, Mark wanted the pre-vis to be able to roll right into production. That way, we could cut together the whole sequence with flat-shaded cities in CG; when we got back to Digital Domain, we could send those tapes out to stage so the crew would not only have a camera curve, but also a visual animation of what the shot was supposed to look like." Beyond establishing camera moves and shot angles, the Prisms pre-visualization helped Stetson and model-shop supervisor Neils Neilson determine the scope of miniature construction. Cunningham set up the software so that he could alter the scale of the buildings without having to reanimate each shot, which enabled Stetson to experiment with various scales until he found the perfect match for the height and width of Digital Domain's motion-control stage. "We modeled the stage environment as well," Stetson explains, "so we could see whether or not our miniature cityscapes would fit into Digital Domain's stage. Then we color-coded the buildings in the pre-vis so we knew when we were past the limits of the stage." "It was a bounding box," Goulekas adds. "As soon as we got 30' or 40' out from the camera and hit the stage floor, everything that got rendered out turned red, so we automatically knew what was CG or a matte painting or miniature buildings." Stetson passed drawings and blueprints back and forth to effects art directors Ron Gress and Ira Gilford to get the cityscape designed and into Digital Domain's model shop, allowing construction to begin before Stetson returned from England. This was a stressful process since Besson and production designer Dan Weil insisted on a retro vision of New York City that would maintain the regimented order of its streets even as it climbed hundreds of stories upward. Some of the structures had to look as if they had been built right on top of older buildings, which would be squared off under reinforced glass cases. “One of Dan Weil’s earliest statements to me was, ‘A Frenchman looks at Manhattan and sees a completely different city structure than a classical European city.’ Unlike Paris or London... New York doesn’t have curves or T-intersections — it’s all about the grid.” — Mark Stetson "Luc and Dan saw our cities differently than any director and production designer I've worked with before," says Stetson, "and I've built New York probably six times! One of Dan Weil's earliest statements to me was, 'A Frenchman looks at Manhattan and sees a completely different city structure than a classical European city.' Unlike Paris or London, with their rat's nests of tiny streets, New York doesn't have curves or T-intersections — it's all about the grid. New York has perspectives to infinity, which corresponded very strongly with Luc Besson's cinematographic style for this picture — center-focused, with one-point perspectives and vanishing points at the crosshairs." Although to creating streets that seemingly stretched on forever proved difficult, Stetson eventually went with 1/24-scale for Element's New York City (the same scale he had employed for The Hudsucker Proxy and The Shadow). "A scale of 1/24 is about as small as you can get for modern cameras," he says. "We had maybe 24 to 30 miniature buildings and they were quite large — up to 24' tall by 40' deep, which filled Digital Domain's main double stage. We had a few shots that were entirely miniature backgrounds, but most of the time we ended up extending the set very creatively with 2-D matte paintings. There were about five shots in which the cityscape was entirely represented by CG. But there was very little live-action production representation in terms of exterior sets for the city. It was left almost entirely to us." With Stetson, Goulekas and Neil still in England shooting the few full-scale setpieces for Leeloo's escape (in which she first sees the enormous New York megalopolis before falling into Korben's cab and leading the police on a chase above and below Times Square), Digital Domain's model shop got the miniatures to stage. "A month after we got back, we started shooting models," Stetson recalls. "For the first time at this facility, Karen was able to actually export camera moves to stage. Instead of it being a one-way street — with motion-control moves recorded and then sent to the CG world — we actually put the pre-vis camera moves into motion-control files. Sometimes that worked as advertised, and sometimes it only panned out to a lesser degree. Next time, we'll make a more accurate CG model of the motion-control axes so we can mimic the camera's moves and limitations more exactly. Still, we created that pathway, and we're proud of it. We looked at each other after it was done and said, 'We actually succeeded!'" The 3-D space in the pre-visualization didn't always relate to the physical stage, but effects cinematographer Bill Neil and his associate, Paul Gentry, found it to be useful, if only as a timing guide. Neil explains, “It was a wonderful communications tool between the visual effects team and the director to help clarify what his vision contained, and to reach a common understanding." Neil insisted on shooting the city background plates before filming the cars in order to create interactive light on the vehicles that would relate to their environment. Although Besson's center-focused perspective ran contrary to Neil's experience in composition, the cameraman found that it offered some interesting problems to solve — such as creating depth when shooting a limited number of buildings being elongated to infinity via digital matte paintings. Adding to this challenge was Besson's directive that the sequence occur in broad daylight. "I was shocked," admits Neil. "Miniatures are often saved by the fact that you don't see much of them, but this whole sequence took place late in the day, and it had to hold up to scrutiny. The stage ceiling was only a few feet above the top of the buildings, so we did tests to see how we could light that expanse of vista. Fortunately, my gaffer, George Ball, and my key grip, Joe Celeste, were able to realize my ideas on the stage. We had a general skylight that came from the top, and then a golden key light that was supposed to be the late day sun raking through any gaps between the buildings. But instead of having key light all coming from roughly the same direction, I took what I came to call a 'fractured light approach,' where I added broken light coming off buildings that were not in frame, often at steep angles, reflecting back in the opposite direction from the sunlight key. That gave the city an energy, a modulation of the light that was quite wonderful. "I also built aerial perspective into the scene so that as the distance from camera increased, the contrast of the scene decreased. Dark things become lighter and light things become darker until ultimately they become monotone. I took that into account in my miniature lighting, and that carried through in the matte painting, which gave us great depth in the streets." Stetson had set some basic specs for the kinds of motion-control rigs needed to shoot down into the canyon of buildings toward the floor: "I wanted to fly the camera from the stage ceiling down into the miniature, so we put the camera on a cruciflex motion-control rig, a motion-control crane with a vertical tower for descent and a horizontal crossbeam for forward motion." Stetson also planned to thread the camera on a 20' extension arm through the miniature streets. At four feet wide, the streets were too narrow for Neil's motion-control camera to run along a horizontal track between the buildings. For the chase's climax, when Korben's cab is pursued below the city through a girder-walled tunnel beneath an underground railroad, Stetson expected Neil to use the extension arm to travel into the 1/6-scale tunnel model. Although the miniature set measured 48', it still wasn't long enough for the high-speed chase. "We made it appear twice as long by shooting it twice, the second time with the camera displaced 48' back," Stetson says. "We needed a very long camera track and two sets of mattes to complete it, but the pieces lined up perfectly with Karen's help. The tunnel chase was almost entirely miniature. Brian Grill some CG debris, some 2-D debris, and smoke resurrected from prior shows to sweeten the scene. The cab is in the foreground, the vanishing point is in the center, and there's a lot of weaving and bobbing." At five minutes and 70-plus shots, the cab-chase sequence represents nearly a third of Digital Domain's Fifth Element effects. Most of the shots involve CG traffic, and sometimes CG hero cabs and police cars. "We digitized the 1/6-scale [models of] Korben's cab and the cop car," Goulekas says. "The hero police car dive down following Leeloo's jump was done in CG because we wanted to see all six surfaces; there was no place to hide an armature mount. You'd be surprised. Our CG traffic pipeline enabled us to say, 'We want a certain percentage of cabs, police cars, red cars, blue cars." The artists worked with low-res versions of the traffic, and then we'd substitute the real traffic on the output on the Tenderer."
  5. Sequence supervisor Sean Cunningham coordinated the creation of all of the pieces for the cab chase, and shader supervisor Simon O' Connor conceived all of the shaders and surfaces for the traffic. But putting all the different elements together fell to compositing supervisor Egstad. "Getting the color balance and the contrast range of all the objects matching properly in daytime was a big challenge," Egstad says. "We composited hundreds of computer graphics cars, which wouldn't fit unless we blurred them a little and boosted their contrast. Also, matte edges aren't nearly as forgiving, so we used a number of different atmospheric tricks, like adding density fog and using filters in Flame to add a nice optical glow from headlights and take the curse off the CG objects." Amid the craziness of production, Stetson was left to his own artistic instincts in terms of directing the development of the cityscape — a potentially dangerous situation. "We'd walk Luc onto the stage when we were shooting, but he'd just look at stuff without any reaction, because he was totally absorbed with directing his actors," Stetson recalls. "Had I been really wrong about what Luc wanted, it would've cost us horribly in terms of time and money. "Finally, when we had traveled way past the point of no return design-wise, I went to his editing suite in Malibu and showed him a color print of a test shot of one of the first big model setups of the city, which represented hundreds of thousands of dollars' worth of work. Up to that point, it was unknown whether he was going to bless it or not. Luc looked at it and his face just lit up completely. He held up the picture to show his editor, Sylvie Landra, pointed to it and then pointed to his face with this big smile on it. He really loved it. That was a huge relief."

评论（默认中文）

prhn 2026/04/09 20:38
我来这里只是为了分享我对这部电影的热爱。我是个超级电影迷。我从高中起就开始看《第五元素》，随着年龄的增长，我越来越欣赏它作为一部电影。

它充满了生机、创造力、色彩、幽默和我们都能联想到的主题（目的、爱、失去等）。

这是布鲁斯·威利斯（Bruce Willis），这部电影还有其他杰出的演员，包括加里·奥德曼（Gary Oldman）和伊恩·霍姆（Ian Holm）。米拉·乔沃维奇（Milla Jovovich）看起来非常有趣，就像一条离开水的鱼，我知道克里斯·塔克（Chris Tucker）在这里扮演的角色并不适合所有人，但在我看来，这很适合这部电影的品牌。几十年来每次都让我崩溃。

大多数情况下，效果已经很好地体现出来了。正如本文所强调的，这通常要归功于大量使用实际效果。

我常常感到悲伤，因为这正在成为一门失传的艺术。拥有大量预算的伟大电影制作人仍在做这种类型的实际效果工作（诺兰[星际穿越]，维伦纽夫[沙丘]），但我认为最终它会随着时间而消失。

I'm just here to share my love for this film. I'm a big movie fan. I've been watching the Fifth Element since high school, and I've only grown to appreciate it more and more as a film as I get older.

It's so full of life, creativity, color, humor, and themes we can all relate to (purpose, love, loss, etc).

This is peek Bruce Willis, and the movie is filled with other exceptional actors including Gary Oldman and Ian Holm. Milla Jovovich is extremely entertaining to watch as a sort fish-out-of-water, and I know Chris Tucker's character here isn't for everyone but in my opinion it's right on-brand for the film. Cracks me up every time for decades.

Mostly the effects have aged really well. That's generally thanks to heavy use of practical effects, as this article highlights.

I often get sad that this is becoming a lost art. Great filmmakers with big budgets are still doing this type of practical effects work (Nolan [Interstellar], Villeneuve [Dune]), but I think eventually it will be lost in time.

他在那部电影中与布鲁斯·威利斯并列前两名。

Yeah this comment to me is incredibly surprising. Chris Tucker played an absolutely incredible character in that movie. So creative, so well executed, so memorable.

He was up there with Bruce Willis as top two in that film.

Such a brilliant movie - and definitely feels like a lost art.

dylan604 2026/04/10 00:32

我欣赏乔治·米勒在《疯狂的麦克斯：狂暴之路》中的另一件事。肯定使用了CG，但很多特技都是真实的，而不是蜘蛛侠级别的废话。

beloch 2026/04/10 00:53
在最近的《疯狂的麦克斯》电影中，米勒使用了CG进行合成，但坚持所有动作都是真实的。 没有CG人把自行车跳到16轮车上。 CG仅用于摆脱安全设备、改变天空等。结果感觉非常真实。
In the recent Mad Max films, Miller used CG for compositing, but insisted that all the action be real. There are no CG people jumping bikes over 16-wheelers. CG was only used to get rid of safety equipment, change the sky, etc.. The results feel viscerally real.

Cthulhu_ 2026/04/09 21:00
> 但我认为最终它会随着时间而消失。

老实说我不相信；模型制作和绘画仍然是数百万人的流行爱好，唯一的问题是电影制片人是否愿意使用它。

最近，尤其是在例如星球大战系列作品中，他们再次使用模型/场景，而不是仅仅使用 CGI 来处理所有事情。

> but I think eventually it will be lost in time.

I don't believe it to be honest; model making and painting remains a popular hobby for millions of people, the only question is whether filmmakers will want to use it.

And recently, especially in e.g. Star Wars franchise entries, they have gone towards using models / sets again instead of just using CGI for everything.

hypercube33 2026/04/10 11:52
CGI 令人惊叹，总是给我作为电影文件制作电影带来巨大的进步，但我绝对不喜欢它在一段时间内取代了电影中的一切。绝对不要看到演员在休息室里试图通过场景和特效呕吐来表演，而不是更好的导演、实际效果和电影的魔力。

我同意，现在我们正在寻找回归平衡的方法，利用一切来讲述故事，而我个人也是为此而努力的。

CGI has amazing and always given me a wonderful advance for film making as a cinefile but I absolutely do not like how it replaced everything in movies for a while. Absolutely slop seeing actors in a green room trying to act through scenes and sfx puke we get instead of better directing, practical effects and magic of movies.

I agree that now we are finding our way back to a balance of using everything together to tell stories and I'm personally here for it.

0x3f 2026/04/09 21:43
但我想知道什么时候数字效果在某种意义上变得“足够好”，以至于它们在包含电影之外看起来永远不会老化。 在某些时候，肯定不会再有任何明显的“解决方案”了。
But I wonder at what point digital effects become 'good enough' in some sense that they never look aged beyond the containing film. At some point surely there's no more perceptible 'resolution' to be had.
在 HN 中查看
peacebeard 2026/04/09 22:35
在实践中，数字效果还没有达到实际效果那样令人信服的程度。在许多情况下，尤其是在自由使用时，数字效果仍然是令人惊叹的数字效果而不是现实。这可能很有趣，但除了认识到 cgi 并不是解决所有问题的最佳解决方案之外，我看不出还有什么其他的进展。
In practice digital effects haven’t approached being convincing the way practical effects do. In many cases, especially when used liberally, digital effects still clock as amazing digital effects rather than reality. It can be enjoyable but I don’t see what would move forward other than recognizing cgi isnt the best solution for everything.
在 HN 中查看
CyberDildonics 2026/04/10 11:07
这不是真的，你只是没有注意到绝大多数的影响。你坐下来观看一部夏季大片，有 1000 个镜头被修改过，几乎所有不是两个人在房间里说话的镜头。

广告试图告诉你“我们做了一切实用的事情！”，这始终是一个谎言，而你却相信了它。

This is not true, you just don't notice the vast majority of effects. You sit down to watch a summer blockbuster, there are 1000 shots that have been altered, pretty much anything that isn't two people talking in a room.

The advertising tries to tell you "we did everything practical!", it's always a lie and you believe it.

steve1977 2026/04/10 04:36
嗯，我的意思是“事情”似乎是 YouTube 上一个人的观点。他当然有权这样做，但我不一定同意。

特别是考虑到他使用 Leeloo 作为“最典型的例子”，但同时也“强调‘昨天天生性感’的比喻通过将女性定位为顺从的而不是平等的伴侣来强化这种动态”，这显然不是真正的情况。

或者例如早期的一个场景，科本试图亲吻她，她用枪指着他的头做出反应，并说“未经我的许可决不许”。对我来说，听起来确实不太无辜或没有代理权。

我明白了分析的要点，它当然不是完全错误的，但说实话，它似乎有点牵强和语无伦次。

Hmm, I mean that "thing" appears to be the opinion of one guy on YouTube. Which he is entitled to of course, but I don't necessarily agree.

Especially considering he's using Leeloo as "the most quintessential example" but then also "emphasizes that the Born Sexy Yesterday trope intensifies the dynamic by positioning women as submissive rather than equal partners", which is clearly not really the case here.

Or for example a scene early on where Korben tries to kiss her, to which she reacts with a gun to his head and says "never without my permission". Doesn't really sound very innocent or without agency to me.

I get the point of the analysis and it's certainly not completely wrong, but it seems to be a bit far-fetched and incoherent to be honest.

Jordan-117 2026/04/09 23:23
优秀的文章。这是分享我最喜欢的科幻世界建筑作品之一的绝佳机会：在起飞过程中用于曼哈顿简短景观的 4K 哑光绘画：

http://web.archive.org/web/20161007133354if_/http://digitald...

这座城市的整体愿景是含蓄的，但却极具创意：海平面显着下降，城市建筑遍布新暴露的土地，留下布鲁克林大桥、曼哈顿的摩天大楼和自由女神像等原始建筑高耸在景观之上。图像中还散布着大量的微小细节——您可以仔细研究它 10 分钟，仍然会发现更多值得欣赏的地方。数字王国最初分享它非常酷。

Excellent article. And a great opportunity to share one of my favorite scifi worldbuilding artifacts: the 4K matte painting used for the brief view of Manhattan during the take-off sequence:

http://web.archive.org/web/20161007133354if_/http://digitald...

The overall vision for the city is implicit but wildly creative: sea levels have dropped significantly, with the architecture of the city spreading across the newly-exposed land and leaving original structures like the Brooklyn Bridge, Manhattan's skyscrapers, and the Statue of Liberty towering above the landscape. There are also oodles of tiny details scattered throughout the image -- you can pore over it for a good 10 minutes and still find more to appreciate. Very cool of Digital Domain to share it originally.

在 HN 中查看
dylan604 2026/04/10 00:45
我的一位摄影朋友正在拍摄城市天际线的全景照片，这些全景照片用于工作室拍摄中填充窗户的哑光绘画的现代版本。亲眼见到他们真是令人印象深刻。

几年前，我参加了华纳兄弟后场的扩展之旅，其中一部分是通过哑光绘画店。其规模非常令人印象深刻。在巡演期间，看到艺术家们在 12 英尺高的梯子上进行创作，真是一次“人性化的规模”。

迪士尼/星球大战的 Favro 在《曼多洛人》中使用的圆形/球形实时屏幕背景也是一种巧妙的技术。

One of the photog friends I have works on shooting panos of city sky lines that are used for the modern version of the matte paintings used to fill the windows in studio shoots. It's impressive to see them in person.

I took the extended WB back lot tour years ago, and part of the tour was through the matte painting shop. The scale is very impressive. To see artists on 12' ladders to work on it was a nice "human for scale" during the tour.

The circular/sphere real time screen backgrounds Favro at Disney/StarWars is using for The Mandolorian is also neat tech as well.

aresant 2026/04/09 22:55
如果你喜欢第五元素，绝对要看佐杜洛夫斯基的沙丘

https://www.imdb.com/title/tt1935156/

封面上讲的是亚历杭德罗·佐杜洛夫斯基 (Alejandro Jodorowsky) 的《沙丘》剧本制作失败的故事，但更深层次的故事是一群令人难以置信的才华横溢的视觉艺术家的聚集，包括让·“莫比乌斯”·吉罗 (Jean "Moebius" Giraud)（在《第五元素》中被提及为核心艺术家）、H.R. 吉格 (H.R. Giger)、克里斯·福斯 (Chris Foss)、萨尔瓦多·达利 (Salvador Dali) 和丹·奥班农 (Dan O'Bannon)。

该团队将继续集中影响大量科幻作品的视觉风格，包括《异形》、《银翼杀手》、《全面回忆》、《星球大战》、《黑客帝国》、《银河护卫队》等。

影片中展现的艺术和创造力绝对是声音的。

有点像最初的 PayPal 黑手党！

If you enjoyed the Fifth Element absolutely watch Jodorowsky's Dune

https://www.imdb.com/title/tt1935156/

On the cover it's a story about the failed production of Alejandro Jodorowsky's Dune script but the deeper story was the aggregation of an unbelievably talented pool of visual artists including Jean "Moebius" Giraud (mentioned as central artist in 5th element), H.R. Giger, Chris Foss, Salvador Dali, & Dan O'Bannon.

That group would go on to centrally influence the visual style of a huge body of science fiction work including Alien, Blade Runner, Total Recall, Star Wars, The Matrix, Guardians of the Galaxy, etc etc.

The art and creativity on display in the film is absolutely sonic.

Kind of like the original PayPal mafia!

I love this movie so much it's _unreal_. What an experience, every single time.

And each time I see an article like this, I simply marvel at the immense love for art and life it has. What an incredibly talented crew, what product of mastery and care.

vidarh 2026/04/09 22:16

nntwozz 2026/04/09 21:14
人们普遍认为《第五元素》和《星际特工：千星之城》拥有相同的主题和风格宇宙，具有相似的美学影响。它们有共同的元素（哈！）和美学，《Valerian》甚至还开设了一家名为“Korbens”的商店作为第五元素的复活节彩蛋。

不幸的是，这部电影不适合我，90 年代是一个更好的时代。

一旦 CGI 成为优秀的讲故事技术，创造力就在好莱坞退居二线。

The Fifth Element and Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets are widely considered to share a thematic and stylistic universe, with similar aesthetic influences. There are shared elements (ha!) and aesthetics, with Valerian even featuring a shop called "Korbens" as an easter egg to The Fifth Element.

Unfortunately the movie doesn't do it for me, the 90s were a better time.

Once CGI became good storytelling and creativity took a backseat in Hollywood.

Joel_Mckay 2026/04/09 18:53
Adam Savage 去年在他的频道上报道了 Mondoshawan 道具：

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pf5dPrmBvwE

这是一部有趣的电影，但克里斯·塔克多次打破了普通观众的节奏。即使现在在烂番茄上，他的角色仍然分散了人们对角色弧线的注意力。

https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/fifth_element

确实是一部邪教经典，但与电影《银翼杀手》相比还差得远。 =3

Adam Savage covered the Mondoshawan props on his channel last year:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pf5dPrmBvwE

It was a fun film, but Chris Tucker broke the pacing too many times for a general audience. Even now on rottentomatoes his role still distracts focus from the character arcs.

https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/fifth_element

Was a cult classic for sure, but nowhere near Blade Runner as a film. =3

在 HN 中查看
NoSalt 2026/04/10 02:03
我非常不同意。克里斯·塔克饰演的红宝石正是这部电影所需要的。当其他人都脸上挂着“这很严肃”的表情时，鲁比就是鲁比，这确实给这部电影增添了一丝轻松感。
I cannot disagree enough. Chris Tucker's Ruby is just what this film needs. With everybody else wearing their "this is serious" face, Ruby being Ruby is a great bit of levity that really adds to this film.
在 HN 中查看
Joel_Mckay 2026/04/10 10:26
这一情节的独特之处在于，科本和佐格在银幕上的斗争中从未真正直接见过对方。 大多数人从来没有注意到这些细节，因为传统的小丑角色经常使观众看不到微妙的东西，因此有些人在混乱中迷失了故事情节。 将鲁迪、加·加·宾克斯或大多数金·凯瑞角色置于荒诞闹剧喜剧之外的类型中，这只是一场糟糕的制作赌博。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bouffon

《银翼杀手》也有自己的问题，但它是一部用更少的资源制作的更好的电影。 =3

What made the plot unique was Korben and Zorg never actually directly met one another in their on-screen struggles. Most never notice such details as a traditional Bouffon character often blinds viewers to subtly, and thus some lose the story arc in the chaos. It is just a poor production gamble to place Rudy, Jar Jar Binks, or most Jim Carrey characters in a genre outside absurdist slapstick comedy.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bouffon

Blade Runner had its own issues, but was a better film done with far less. =3

在 HN 中查看
cubefox 2026/04/09 23:14
我认为，如果贝松淡化了偶尔夸张的愚蠢行为，比如克里斯·塔克的角色，或者邪恶的宇宙飞船（文章中描述的球体），这感觉就像是从电影中取出的科幻模仿，我认为这部电影会更好（尽管可能不太成功） 太空球。

莫比斯的节奏、出色的服装和布景设计、演员布鲁斯·威利斯和米拉·乔沃维奇，以及不寻常的想法（比如外星歌剧歌手）都足以支撑这部电影。

I think the film would have been better (though perhaps less successful) if Besson had toned down the occasionally exaggerated tomfoolery, like Chris Tucker's character, or the spaceship Evil (the orb described in the article) which felt almost like a SciFi parody taken out of the movie Spaceballs.

The pacing, the great costumes and set design by Moebius, the actors Bruce Willis and Milla Jovovich, and the unusual ideas (like the alien opera singer) were all more than enough to carry the movie.

在 HN 中查看

在 HN 中查看
vidarh 2026/04/09 22:13
《第五元素》几乎是贝松在获得《星际特工》的资金之前就开始制作《星际特工》的作品，所以我们确实得到了某种精神续集。

不幸的是，虽然我越来越喜欢《星际特工》这部电影，但与《第五元素》相比，贝松应该为《星际特工》获得更严格的预算，而不是他获得的明显近乎自由的统治。

Fifth Element is pretty much Besson doing Valerian before he was able to get funding for Valerian, so we kinda did get a spiritual sequel of sorts.

Unfortunately, while I've grown to like the Valerian movie, when compared to Fifth Element it would seem that Besson should have been given a far tighter budget for Valerian rather than the apparent near free reign he got.

jr3592 2026/04/10 01:44
你在哪里看到这些？ 下次去巴黎我很想去。

在 HN 中查看
Sam6late 2026/04/10 13:35
它是法国公司的总部，阿尔斯通的法国总部位于巴黎附近的塞纳河畔圣旺，地址：48 rue Albert Dhalenne, 93400 Saint-Ouen, France。展览展示了吕克·贝松 1997 年电影《第五元素》中的原创道具，包括科本·达拉斯 (Korben Dallas) 著名的黄色飞行出租车和蓝银色纽约警察局警车。阿尔斯通将这些作品展出，以有趣的方式致敬他们对城市交通的共同关注。
It was the HQ of French company, Alstom's French headquarters is located in Saint-Ouen-sur-Seine, near Paris, at 48 rue Albert Dhalenne, 93400 Saint-Ouen, France. The display shows original props from Luc Besson’s 1997 movie The Fifth Element — including Korben Dallas’s famous yellow flying taxi and the blue-and-silver NYPD police car. Alstom keeps these pieces on display as a fun tribute to their shared focus on city mobility.

ModernMech 2026/04/09 19:10
这篇文章缺少整部电影中最好的未来场景镜头之一！

http://i.imgur.com/6W5InkH.jpg

这张图片只在屏幕上出现了大约 2 秒钟，但它讲述了一个完整的故事，真正把我带入了电影中。前半部分你在城市深处，最后当你从远处看时，它看起来就像是一个完整的真实城市，而不是他们拍摄的几个场景。也让它感觉像是我们未来的连续性，而不是一些随机的外星戏剧。

The article is missing one of the best futurescape shots in the whole movie!

http://i.imgur.com/6W5InkH.jpg

That image is only on screen for like 2 seconds, but it tells a whole story and really pulled me into the film. The first half you're deep in the city, and then finally when you get to see it from afar, it seems like a whole real city instead of the few locales they shot. Also makes it feel like a continuity of our future instead of some random alien drama.

gwern 2026/04/10 01:00
但就是 电影摄影 有什么兴趣吗？为什么OP要包含它？他们谈论的是硬镜头，比如爆炸的宇宙飞船，他们需要在沙漠中找到一个地点向其发射数十枚迫击炮，或者疯狂的蓝色油漆需要特殊的紫外线曝光才能渲染得恰到好处。看起来像是……哑光画？当然，一幅漂亮的哑光画对于世界构建很重要。但仅此而已。
But is the cinematography there of any interest? Why would OP include it? They're talking about the hard shots, like the exploding spaceship where they need to find a spot in the desert to shoot dozens of mortars at it, or the crazy blue paint needing special UV light exposures to render just right. That looks like... a matte painting? A nice matte painting, sure, important for worldbuilding. But just that.
在 HN 中查看
ModernMech 2026/04/10 01:36
您可能是对的，《美国电影摄影师》杂志对这个镜头不感兴趣，这就是它不包括在内的原因。我仍然认为这是电影中最好的未来场景镜头，将前半部分的其余部分很好地结合在一起。
You're probably right that the shot isn't of interest to American Cinematographer magazine, which is why it's not included. I still think it's the best futurescape shot in the movie, serving to tie the rest of the first half together nicely.

tomaytotomato 2026/04/09 19:02
我对《第五元素》有着美好的回忆，这是我在 9 至 10 岁的时候被允许在电影院观看的第一部 PG-13 电影之一。

回想起来，整个故事给人一种与平常阴暗的、被污染的反乌托邦地球不同的未来感，有点“近未来”的感觉。

看到悬浮汽车驶过麦当劳将永远是我未来的希望（我内心10岁的自己）

I have fond memories of the Fifth Element, as one of my first PG-13 movies at the cinema that I was allowed to see as a 9-10 year old.

Looking back, the whole story gives a different futuristic feel to the usual gloomy polluted dystopian earths, and feels a bit, "near-future".

Seeing hover cars getting drive through McDonalds will forever be a future hope for me (my inner 10 year old self)
